author: @benthere8051
text: Dr. Smith, I can see you would really enjoy microwave stripline design. You can make capacitors, inductors, transformers, couplers and many other components with simple patterns on a PC board. It would be really interesting to make a plated-thru hole on your paper PC board with a paper punch and ink on both sides. Your ink would lend itself very well to silk-screening, as you said.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am going to look into it mate - sounds like my kind of thing for sure

author: @4gauge10
text: I wonder if that graphene ink could be used in a CNC graghics🖨⬅printer to print precise and very fine active circuitry on paper?.

replies.author: @corresandberg
replies.text: Or add it in a re-fill cartridge and print it using a standard HP or Canon inkjet printer :)

replies.author: @4gauge10
replies.text: @corresandberg Now,that would work too,but you can't do graphics with those.

replies.author: @waynegnarlie1
replies.text: That is exactly what I'm planning to do with the graphene ink I just ordered from Rob. .  Then use conductive adheshive to hold and connect components.  The equivalent of plated thru-holes might be done simply by punching tiny holes in the paper and dropping-on some graphene ink at each hole after both sides are printed.  We'll see if it works....it is an experiment.  If it works it would be a huge benefit, and likely replace other methods of making homemade PCBs.

replies.author: @4gauge10
replies.text: @waynegnarlie1 If it works,make a video to show us,it sounds like a very practical idea to me.

replies.author: @waynegnarlie1
replies.text: @4gauge10 Will do for sure.
